claim: There are four types of AI evaluations: code-based (string checks), human evaluation (manual thumbs up/down), LLM-as-a-judge (scaled human-like labeling), and user evaluation (real-world feedback)
actionable: Structure your eval strategy using all four types: start with code-based checks for basics, add human labels for validation, scale with LLM-as-a-judge, and monitor user feedback
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Start with 10 human-labeled test examples for internal evaluation, then scale to 100+ examples before production; this balances iteration speed with confidence
actionable: Begin development with 10 manually-labeled examples; iterate on prompts quickly at this scale; once you have stable prompts, expand to 100+ examples for production confidence
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Product managers must personally do the human labeling and evaluation spreadsheet work, not delegate it to contractors; PM judgment is critical for defining what 'good' means
actionable: Don't outsource human evaluation labeling entirely; spend time yourself grading outputs to internalize product standards and make better eval criteria decisions
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Build evaluation rubrics by defining what's 'good', 'average', and 'bad' for each criteria dimension before generating any test data
actionable: Create a rubric spreadsheet with specific definitions for each rating level (good/average/bad) for each criterion; share this with team and debate until aligned
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Use Anthropic's Workbench to generate initial prompts from high-level requirements, then customize; this accelerates prompt development from zero
actionable: Use Anthropic Workbench's prompt generation feature to bootstrap chatbot prompts; describe your use case and it generates initial prompt with variables and examples
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: LLM evals must be validated against human labels; measure agreement rate (match score) between LLM judge and human labels to determine if eval is trustworthy
actionable: Before trusting LLM-as-a-judge on 100 examples, validate it on 10-20 human-labeled examples; compute match rate between LLM and human verdicts
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: When eval is good but user feedback is bad, it indicates either misaligned eval criteria or a user satisfaction signal unrelated to technical correctness; go back and review the data
actionable: If metrics pass but users complain (or vice versa), investigate whether your eval criteria actually measure user satisfaction; don't assume metrics are truth
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Iterate on prompts 20+ times with the same test set; each iteration should be informed by specific evaluation feedback from your rubric
actionable: Don't expect prompts to improve in 2-3 iterations; plan for 20+ cycles of: modify prompt → test on 10 examples → review rubric mismatches → adjust criteria or prompt
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Extract context (product info, policies) from your actual website/docs and inject directly into the prompt; don't rely on the model to know your company details
actionable: Embed company-specific data (product catalog, policy docs, SOP) directly in the prompt as context variables; don't expect the model to learn this from training
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Collect notes on why each example failed; use these notes to identify patterns and guide future prompt improvements or policy changes
actionable: Add a notes column to your evaluation spreadsheet; document why each 'bad' or 'average' example failed; summarize patterns to guide product/prompt updates
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Debate eval criteria with your team on edge cases; healthy disagreement about borderline ratings leads to better eval criteria and product understanding
actionable: In your first evaluation pass, flag borderline cases and have your team discuss: is this policy compliant or not? What does the rubric say? Refine rubric based on debate
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Avoid completely delegating eval labor to contractors; PMs directly labeling data is the fastest path to discovering what's actually wrong with your system
actionable: Do your own eval labeling, at least for the first 10-20 examples; this forces you to confront misaligned requirements and leads to faster problem discovery
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Tone, policy compliance, and product knowledge are the three key rubric dimensions for customer support chatbots; define these clearly for consistent labeling
actionable: For support chatbots, build your initial rubric with these three criteria: does it know our products? Does it follow policy? Does it match our tone?
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: LLMs are particularly bad at math; common failure mode is claiming 45 minutes exceeds a 60-minute window; always test edge cases where calculation or comparison is needed
actionable: Add test cases to your eval set that require basic math or numeric comparison; flag any failures here as high priority for prompt improvement
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: When policy information is missing or ambiguous, agent may deflect to 'contact support' instead of being direct; update policy docs to address edge cases
actionable: If your evals reveal agents frequently suggesting 'contact support', review whether your policy docs actually cover those edge cases or if you need explicit handling rules
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan

claim: Use evaluation platforms like Arize to replay prompt variations across your test set at scale, replacing manual testing of each example individually
actionable: Once you have 10+ test examples, upload them to a platform like Arize or similar; use it to test multiple prompt versions and models in parallel
source_title: Complete Beginner's Course on AI Evaluations in 50 Minutes (2025) | Aman Khan
